content.text:
*"For which of you, desiring to build a tower, does not 
first sit down and count the cost?"*{Luke 14:28}{We may say most aptly that the Analytical Engine weaves algebraical patterns just as the Jacquard loom weaves flowers and leaves.}{Ada Lovelace}

An **algorithm** is a finite sequence of well-defined 
instructions for solving a problem or performing a computation.

content.title: Algorithm
content.content:
An **algorithm** is a finite, ordered sequence of unambiguous, 
executable steps that terminates after a finite amount of time, producing 
a result or output.

items: **Input**: Zero or more inputs from a specified set.
**Output**: One or more outputs related to the inputs.
**Definiteness**: Each step is precisely and unambiguously defined.
**Finiteness**: The algorithm terminates after finitely many steps.
**Effectiveness**: Each step is basic enough to be carried out.

content.title: Muhammad ibn Musa al-Khwarizmi (c. 780--850)
content.content:
The word "algorithm" derives from the Latinized name of the Persian 
mathematician al-Khwarizmi, whose treatise on arithmetic introduced 
Hindu-Arabic numerals to the West. His book on algebra (*al-Kitab 
al-mukhtasar fi hisab al-jabr wal-muqabala*) gave us the word "algebra." 
Al-Khwarizmi's systematic methods for solving equations exemplified 
algorithmic thinking a millennium before computers.

content.text: Consider the problem: Given a list of numbers, find the largest one.

content.title: Maximum-Finding Algorithm
content.content:
1. Set $\text{max} \gets$ first element of the list.

2. For each remaining element $x$ in the list:
 enumerate

3. If $x > \text{max}$, set $\text{max} \gets x$.
 Return $\text{max}$.
enumerate

content.title: Tracing the maximum algorithm
content.problem:
Find the maximum of $[3, 7, 2, 9, 4]$.

| Step | Current element | max |
| 1 | 3 | 3 (initial) |
| 2 | 7 | 7 (updated: $7 > 3$) |
| 3 | 2 | 7 (unchanged: $2 \not> 7$) |
| 4 | 9 | 9 (updated: $9 > 7$) |
| 5 | 4 | 9 (unchanged: $4 \not> 9$) |

Output: 9.

content.text:
An algorithm is **correct** if it produces the 
correct output for every valid input. Proving correctness often involves:

items: **Loop invariants**: properties that hold at each iteration.
**Termination**: proof that loops and recursion eventually stop.

content.title: Loop invariant for maximum
content.problem:
**Invariant**: After processing $k$ elements, $\text{max}$ contains 
the largest of those $k$ elements.

**Initialization**: After 1 element, max equals that element (correct).

**Maintenance**: If the invariant holds for $k$ elements, then after 
processing element $k+1$, max is updated if necessary, maintaining the 
invariant.

**Termination**: After all $n$ elements, max is the largest of all.

marginNotes.text:
In their hearts humans plan their course, 
but the Lord establishes their steps.
